Summary of what was found: Berlin Packaging’s resin guide shows why PET, HDPE, LDPE, and PP form the mainstream rigid and squeezable-material baseline for beauty and personal care primary packs.
Detailed description of what was found: The source states that PET offers gloss, clarity, impact resistance, and is desirable for clear bottles and jars; LDPE is flexible and suitable for squeezable packaging such as shampoo, conditioners, and lotions; HDPE is widely used for extrusion-blown bottles because it is economical, impact resistant, and offers a moisture barrier; and PP provides moisture barrier and high-temperature stability. This supports using PET, HDPE, LDPE, and PP as baseline material families for later lightweighting comparisons in bottles, jars, tubes, and closures.
Source name: About Plastic Resins for Packaging — Berlin Packaging — https://www.berlinpackaging.com/about-plastic-resins/

Summary of what was found: APC Packaging confirms the current beauty primary-packaging material set: PP, PE, PET, PETG/PCTA/PCTG, PMMA/acrylic, AS/SAN, ABS, aluminum, and glass.
Detailed description of what was found: This source maps materials directly to cosmetic packaging use. It explains PP as heat and chemical resistant; PE as ranging from soft and flexible to hard and sturdy; PET as clear and chemically resistant; PETG/PCTA/PCTG as offering above-average chemical resistance and clarity compared with clarified PP; PMMA/acrylic as lightweight, shatter-resistant plastic glass commonly used as an outer jar or bottle; AS/SAN as rigid, strong, tough, and transparent; ABS as high-gloss, impact resistant, and easy to decorate; aluminum as luxurious and protective; and glass as highly chemically resistant with strong barrier properties. It supports the report’s format-material baseline and premium-versus-mass distinction.
Source name: Packaging Materials For Cosmetic Containers — APC Packaging — https://apcpackaging.com/custom-services/materials/

Summary of what was found: Quadpack explains that PET injection molding enables greater wall thickness than blow molding to create rigid beauty packs with a luxurious look and feel.
Detailed description of what was found: This source connects PET to premium-style execution rather than only mass-market packaging. Quadpack states that blow molding is used for thin-walled products, while injection molding allows greater wall thicknesses to create products with a luxurious look and feel. It also notes PET compatibility with many formulations and describes constraints when moving to rPET due to shape and aesthetic factors. This makes heavy-wall PET a major premium primary-packaging baseline and a likely lightweighting target.
Source name: The art of PET injection moulding — Quadpack — https://www.quadpack.com/assets/13873460/the-art-of-pet-injection-moulding/

Summary of what was found: Quadpack’s Cloud Jars show that premium skincare jars still use heavy, thick-wall glass to deliver a high-end aesthetic.
Detailed description of what was found: Quadpack describes Cloud Jars as single-wall glass jars with a premium look, heavy glass construction, and thick-wall glass for a high-end finish. It also mentions compatibility with creams and moisturizers and multiple decoration options. This is strong evidence that heavy glass jars remain a current baseline in prestige skincare and represent a high-weight starting point for lightweight-material substitution analysis.

Summary of what was found: Ball’s Infinity aluminum bottle line confirms that aluminum bottles are used in beauty and personal care and can combine lightweighting, monomateriality, customization, and a premium aesthetic.
Detailed description of what was found: Ball states that its impact-extruded aluminum bottle line is customizable for beauty and personal care products, monomaterial, and lightweight, saving transport energy. The product positioning also frames aluminum bottles as sophisticated and high-end. This supports including aluminum bottles in the primary-packaging baseline for selected beauty and personal care applications, especially as a lighter alternative to glass with premium design cues.
Source name: A Circular Alternative to Plastic Packaging — Ball Corporation — https://investors.ball.com/news-presentations/press-releases/detail/165/a-circular-alternative-to-plastic-packaging

Summary of what was found: Quadpack’s Trap PET Jar shows a common mixed-material jar architecture: PET jar, PP cap, and PE shive.
Detailed description of what was found: This source gives an explicit beauty jar bill of materials: jar in PET, cap in PP, and shive in PE. It also states that virgin PET is ideal for highlighting formula texture, while PCR PET is more convenient for solid-color applications such as supplements and nutricosmetics. This is strong evidence that many current jars are not mono-material and that PET/PP/PE combinations are common in beauty jars, which matters for both weight and recyclability trade-offs.

Summary of what was found: Quadpack’s Regula Airless shows that premium-looking airless packs often combine multiple rigid plastics, including SAN, PET or rPET, and PP, plus a pump system.
Detailed description of what was found: Quadpack describes Regula Airless as popular among beauty brands because of its premium look and high-quality performance. It specifies a double-wall architecture with the outer bottle and cap in SAN, PET, or rPET and the inner in PP, plus a metal-free pump. This supports using multi-resin airless packs as a current baseline format for premium skincare and highlights where lightweighting or material simplification efforts may focus.

Summary of what was found: Aptar’s GSA Advance shows a recyclable next-generation lotion pump made as an all-polyolefin system using PP and PE for skin care, personal care, and color cosmetics.
Detailed description of what was found: Aptar describes GSA Advance as an all-plastic lotion pump and all-polyolefin pump made from polypropylene and polyethylene, designed to maximize recyclability while maintaining performance and design options from premium to lifestyle. This clarifies dispenser material reality: many dispensers are multi-component systems, while the future direction is PP/PE-based mono-family construction aligned with bottle materials.

Summary of what was found: Silgan Dispensing states that conventional dispensers contain non-recyclable metals and materials and contrasts them with 100% polyolefin, no-metal-spring systems.
Detailed description of what was found: Silgan says its LifeCycle technology removes non-recyclable metals and materials used in conventional dispensers, enabling packs to be made nearly completely from a single polyolefin. It also says ReVive 2cc has no metal spring and improves compatibility with PET, HDPE, and PP bottle recycling, while Pearl 2 uses a patented plastic spring and 100% polyolefin design. This supports treating pumps and dispenser mechanisms as a distinct hotspot in lightweighting and mono-material redesign.
Source name: CMPL Expo 2026 — Silgan Dispensing Systems — https://silgandispensing.com/cmpl-expo/

Summary of what was found: Gerresheimer’s cosmetic glass dropper catalogue confirms glass as a current standard for serum/oil droppers and provides actual pack weights by capacity.
Detailed description of what was found: Gerresheimer lists cosmetic glass dropper bottles from 5 ml to 100 ml and shows weights rising from 20 g at 5 ml to 47 g at 30 ml, 55 g at 50 ml, and 100 g at 100 ml. This provides concrete evidence that glass droppers are weight-intensive primary packs and should be priority candidates for lightweighting analysis, especially in prestige skincare categories where glass droppers are common.
Source name: Dropper bottle for specialities made of moulded glass — Gerresheimer — https://www.gerresheimer.com/catalogue/products/cosmetics-fragrance/glass-packaging/others/dropper-bottles.html

Summary of what was found: APC Packaging’s mono-material dropper shows that traditional droppers are typically multi-material and often use glass, while APC’s redesign uses enhanced PP for both bottle and pipette.
Detailed description of what was found: APC states that typical market droppers are multi-material and non-recyclable and that its redesign replaces the usual glass-based approach with an enhanced polypropylene bottle and pipette that can be recycled as one unit. This supports using glass bottle plus mixed-material dropper as the current baseline and all-PP dropper systems as a lightweight, recyclability-oriented future benchmark.
Source name: Mono-Material Dropper with Bottle - Recycle Code 5 — APC Packaging — https://apcpackaging.com/mono-material-dropper/

Summary of what was found: Berlin Packaging shows why squeeze tubes remain common in beauty and personal care: they are lightweight, durable, leak-resistant, portable, and easy to decorate.
Detailed description of what was found: Berlin says brands choose plastic squeeze tubes for durability, resistance to breakage and leakage, lightweighting, dispensing ease, product protection, shelf life, and customizable graphics. It also notes that recyclable mono-material toothpaste-style tubes use multiple layers of HDPE with EVOH rather than an aluminum foil layer, and that tube work can be done in mono-material PP or HDPE constructions. This supports PE/HDPE-based tubes as a mainstream lightweight route while flagging barrier-layer complexity as a design constraint.
Source name: The Latest Twists and Turns in Plastic Squeeze Tubes — Berlin Packaging — https://www.berlinpackaging.com/insights/product-categories/latest-twists-and-turns-in-plastic-squeeze-tubes

Summary of what was found: ALLTUB confirms that aluminum squeeze tubes remain a mainstream option for cosmetics needing high barrier and hygiene.
Detailed description of what was found: ALLTUB states that aluminum tubes have exceptional protective barrier properties, including airtightness, no ingress of light and UV rays, heat resistance, and corrosion resistance, and that they are widely used for pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and foods. The companion market page adds that aluminum and laminate tubes are well-suited to sensitive and demanding cosmetic formulas. This places aluminum tubes in the high-protection, higher-material-intensity, premium or specialist-use portion of the tube-material baseline.

Summary of what was found: Albéa’s Metamorphosis is described as a carton-based cosmetic tube where plastic is reduced and replaced with bio-based, certified paper-like material.
Detailed description of what was found: This source supports the paper-based primary-packaging baseline. Paper-based tubes remain a minority format compared with PE and laminate tubes, but Albéa’s product shows that paper-heavy cosmetic tubes are already commercialized as a current material pathway. It justifies including paper-based tubes as a secondary but relevant current baseline for later lightweighting and sustainability comparison, especially for brands exploring lower-plastic premium storytelling.

Summary of what was found: APC’s color-cosmetics compact page shows that compact cases are commonly built from aesthetic rigid plastics such as ABS and SAN.
Detailed description of what was found: APC lists the YYD3123 round compact as Compact ABS/SAN, confirming the use of these rigid plastics in compact shells. Combined with APC’s broader material guide, this supports using ABS-, SAN-, and PMMA-based compacts as the current baseline for face powders and similar formats in color cosmetics, with attention to decoration, clarity, and premium tactile feel rather than minimum weight.

Summary of what was found: Mondi’s mono-material StandUpPouch confirms that PE-based refill pouches are already current solutions for liquid soap and detergent refills, with optional EVOH or PCR depending on application and barrier need.
Detailed description of what was found: Mondi states that its pouch is a PE-based mono-material solution, recyclable where facilities exist, and can include EVOH or PCR depending on the application. It also says the pouch replaces non-recyclable laminates and is used for liquid soap and detergent refills. This supports using mono-PE refill pouches as a realistic lightweight baseline while recognizing that barrier add-ons such as EVOH can complicate material purity and recyclability.

Summary of what was found: Amcor’s AmPrima range shows that home and personal care refills are increasingly built in PE- and PP-format recycle-ready pouches that retain barrier, stiffness, seal strength, graphics performance, and high-speed runnability.
Detailed description of what was found: Amcor states that AmPrima is available in PE and PP formats, is designed to preserve the performance of traditional unrecyclable packaging, and can deliver up to 90% packaging weight reduction for refill products versus rigid containers. It also notes PCR-content options. This is strong evidence that flexible refill packs already form a current primary-packaging baseline in personal care and are central to material-based lightweighting.
Source name: AmPrima Recycle-Ready Packaging for home and personal care — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/sustainability/products/amprima/emea/homeandpersonalcare

Summary of what was found: Amcor’s AmFiber page confirms that paper-based primary sachets are already used for single-use home and personal care applications such as face masks, serums, shampoo, and conditioner.
Detailed description of what was found: Amcor describes AmFiber as a curbside-recyclable paper-based sachet for difficult-to-hold aqueous products and explicitly names face masks, serums, shampoo, and conditioner. It highlights barrier protection, machinery compatibility, shelf appeal, and premiumization. This makes it a relevant baseline reference for paper-led primary packs in sample, single-dose, and travel-size beauty applications.
Source name: Product Sheet: AmFiber Paper-Based Home and Personal Care Sachets - North America — Amcor — https://www.amcor.com/insights/educational-resources/paperbased-hpc-sachets
